card.intuition: Git stores your project history as a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of objects (commits, trees, blobs), where each commit points to its parent(s) via a unique SHA-1 hash.
card.analogy: A family tree that never loops: you can have one child with two parents (a Merge commit), or one parent with many children (Branches). But you can never be your own grandfather (Acyclic).
when_to_use: Understanding why 'Merge Conflicts' occur at a structural level
Using `git rev-parse` or `git cat-file` to debug corrupted repositories
Visualizing complex branch histories with `git log --graph`
gotchas: Git is 'Content-Addressable': if you change one byte in a file, its hash changes, which changes the tree hash, which changes the commit hash. History is immutable!
Branches are just lightweight pointers to a commit hash. When you move a branch, you aren't moving the history, just the sticker.

exercise.prompt: Identify the data structure Git uses to represent commit history.
exercise.test_code:
def check(ans): return ans.upper() == "DAG"
assert check("DAG")
print("PASS")
